title: Rosa × insignis
intro: Rosa × insignis, also known as the wild rose or Scottish rose, is a hybrid rose species that is native to the United Kingdom. This plant has been used in traditional medicine for its potential health benefits, including anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties, which may help to alleviate symptoms of digestive issues, arthritis, and other inflammatory conditions. The bioactive constituents of Rosa × insignis include flavonoids, phenolic acids, and terpenoids, which are responsible for its medicinal properties. The most common herbal preparations made with Rosa × insignis include rose petal tea, rosehip syrup, and rose oil, which are often used to promote relaxation, soothe skin irritations, and support immune function.
taxonomy: Rosa × insignis is classified within the family Rosaceae, and it is a hybrid of Rosa gallica and Rosa moschata. This classification places it within the order Rosales. The genus Rosa × insignis is further classified into the subfamily Rosoideae. The species is often grouped into sections based on characteristics, with some classifications placing it in the section Rosa. Further, it is classified as a rose species within the species group of old garden roses.
